title: Sanction Policy — Privileged Abuse on EHR Audit Logs

compliance.assurance_rationale: HIPAA Security §164.308(a)(1)(ii)(C) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that HIPAA Security §164.308(a)(1)(ii)(C) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.10.12: Sanction Policy — Privileged Abuse on EHR Audit Logs.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.10.12' running on sourcetype epic:audit and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742)
spl:
index=epic sourcetype="epic:audit" AccessType="PatientChart" earliest=-30d
| lookup employee_dependents.csv employee_id as USER_ID OUTPUT dependent_patient_hash
| eval pat_hash=sha256(PAT_ID)
| where pat_hash=dependent_patient_hash
| stats count by USER_ID, PAT_ID
| where count>=1
description: Detects workforce sanctions triggers such as repeated self-family lookups or audit log tampering attempts by IT admins supporting HIPAA sanction policy enforcement.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for HIPAA Security compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes epic and sourcetypes epic:audit, epic:audit`. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Use salted hashes only; (2) legal/privacy approval for dependent mapping; (3) integrate HR sanctions workflow; (4) false positive review for legitimate care team overlap. Indexes required: `index=epic`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=epic:audit`, `sourcetype=epic:audit``; Lookups: `employee_dependents`, `employee_dependents.csv`.
visualization: Table (user, event count), Link analysis graph (user-patient edges). Key fields: `PAT_ID`, `USER_ID`, `pat_hash`.
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
grandmaExplanation: We watch for detects workforce sanctions triggers such as repeated self-family lookups or audit log tampering attempts by it admins supporting hipaa sanction policy enforcement. This is critical because gaps here could mean serious regulatory exposure or security blind spots.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=epic` `sourcetype=epic:audit` (`USER_ID`, `PAT_ID`, `AccessType`), HR relationship file `employee_dependents.csv` (hashed IDs).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Use salted hashes only; (2) legal/privacy approval for dependent mapping; (3) integrate HR sanctions workflow; (4) false positive review for legitimate care team overlap.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=epic sourcetype="epic:audit" AccessType="PatientChart" earliest=-30d
| lookup employee_dependents.csv employee_id as USER_ID OUTPUT dependent_patient_hash
| eval pat_hash=sha256(PAT_ID)
| where pat_hash=dependent_patient_hash
| stats count by USER_ID, PAT_ID
| where count>=1
```

Understanding this SPL

**Sanction Policy — Privileged Abuse on EHR Audit Logs (§164.308(a)(1)(ii)(C))** — Detects workforce sanctions triggers such as repeated self-family lookups or audit log tampering attempts by IT admins supporting HIPAA sanction policy enforcement.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=epic` `sourcetype=epic:audit` (`USER_ID`, `PAT_ID`, `AccessType`), HR relationship file `employee_dependents.csv` (hashed IDs). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: epic; **sourcetype**: epic:audit. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=epic, sourcetype="epic:audit", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **pat_hash** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where pat_hash=dependent_patient_hash` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by USER_ID, PAT_ID** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Filters the current rows with `where count>=1` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (user, event count), Link analysis graph (user-patient edges).
